findings.title: Brief gives March 14, 2021 incident date, but file sources give March 12, 2021
findings.description: The location and general description are corroborated, but the brief's date of March 14, 2021 conflicts with the medical, police, and witness documents, all of which state March 12, 2021. The incompatible words are “March 14, 2021” versus “March 12, 2021.”

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006

I, Diane Tran, provide the following statement of my own free will regarding the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is verbatim and repeated: the brief states March 14, while medical, police, and witness sources all state March 12 for the same incident and location. This is mechanically checkable from the quoted dates.

findings.title: Time-bar section states March 14 incident date contrary to March 12 file sources
findings.description: Every source document that dates the incident places it on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021. The date stated in the brief is therefore incompatible with the record.

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
DATE OF ADMISSION:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
regarding the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is mechanically established by the quoted dates: the brief says March 14, while medical, police, and witness records all say March 12. This occurrence is especially material because it appears in the limitations section.

findings.title: Brief repeats March 14, 2021 incident date contrary to March 12, 2021 file sources
findings.description: The source documents corroborate that Rivera was working on scaffolding at the project when a section gave way, but they contradict the stated date. The brief says “March 14, 2021,” while the medical and police records state “March 12, 2021.”
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 3

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Patient was working on scaffolding at a commercial construction site when the structure gave way.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
A section approximately 12 feet in width had given way.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The same scaffolding-collapse event is described in both the brief and source documents, but the dates are incompatible: March 14 in the brief versus March 12 in medical and police records. The matching event details make the contradiction unambiguous.

findings.title: Brief says Rivera lacked required PPE, but file says he wore hard hat and harness
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement directly state he was wearing a hard hat and harness, and that the lanyard detached because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s claim that Rivera lacked PPE is directly contradicted by both the police report and witness statement, which identify a hard hat, harness, and high-visibility vest. The lanyard evidence also directly rebuts the implication that no fall-arrest equipment was used.

findings.title: Time-barred heading contradicted by brief's concession that filing falls within limitations window
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading says the claims are time-barred, while the quoted body text expressly concedes the filing falls nominally within the limitations window. The exact limitations calculation partly relies on legal/arithmetic assumptions, but the internal inconsistency is plain from the brief excerpts.

findings.title: Exclusive Apex responsibility framing is undercut by evidence of Harmon foreman control
findings.description: The documents support that Apex was Rivera’s employer, but the claim’s framing that Apex, “not Harmon,” was responsible for scaffolding operations is undercut by evidence that Harmon’s foreman directed Rivera’s crew to work on the east-side scaffold and dismissed a specific safety concern. Those facts conflict with the brief’s exclusive-responsibility assertion.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Here, the undisputed facts establish that Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Employer:      Apex Staffing Solutions
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
During our morning coordination meeting, Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record quotes do not deny Apex was Rivera’s employer, but they directly undermine the brief’s exclusive 'Apex — not Harmon' responsibility framing by showing Harmon’s foreman directed the work and dismissed a safety concern. Some interpretation is required because responsibility can be shared, but the conflict is material.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette is a real and well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted sentence is materially too absolute. Privette did not announce that a hirer is “never liable” whenever the injury arises from contracted work; its holding concerned peculiar-risk/vicarious liability to an independent contractor’s employee in light of workers’ compensation. The absolute wording conflicts with the Privette doctrine as later and commonly understood, which is a presumption/no-duty rule subject to recognized exceptions. Known actual language/rule: Privette bars, in general, a contractor’s employee from using the peculiar-risk doctrine to impose vicarious tort liability on the person who hired the contractor, especially where the hirer did not itself cause the injury. California’s later Privette-line cases recognize exceptions, including retained-control/affirmative-contribution and concealed-hazard theories.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted 'never liable' formulation is strongly inconsistent with the canonical Privette line and its recognized exceptions, making the overstatement substantial. Confidence is below mechanical certainty because the actual Privette opinion text is not supplied in the evidence and the finding relies partly on legal knowledge.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated proposition: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
findings.description: Seabright is about delegation of workplace-safety duties under the Privette doctrine, not a general evidentiary rule that compliance with statutory safety requirements is “highly probative” of due care. The brief’s broader use—especially the claim that an unblemished OSHA record effectively insulates Harmon from tort liability—overstates and mischaracterizes Seabright.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: Seabright’s role in the Privette delegation doctrine is well established, and using it as a broad OSHA-compliance due-care rule is a material doctrinal stretch. Confidence is capped because the actual brief proposition and Seabright text are not quoted in full in the supplied evidence.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize this Ninth Circuit case, and there are strong fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is more consistent with late-1980s Federal Reporter volumes than a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation, and the quoted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspiciously broad and tailored. I am not aware of a general Ninth Circuit/California rule creating a rebuttable presumption of due care from OSHA compliance. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California negligence law.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The reporter-volume/year mismatch and unusually broad OSHA presumption are meaningful red flags, but the finding still depends on non-recognition rather than a conclusive source check. If the citation is fabricated it would be a serious misrepresentation supporting a central argument, which justifies the high severity despite only moderate confidence.

findings.title: Elapsed-time calculation uses contradicted incident date and appears arithmetically wrong
findings.description: The supplied sources do not verify the complaint filing date, but they do contradict the incident date used in the elapsed-time calculation. From the source-supported incident date of March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is one year and 363 days, not “one year and 362 days”; even using the brief’s March 14, 2021 date, March 10, 2023 is one year and 361 days after the incident by ordinary exclusive-day counting.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record-supported incident date is March 12, not the brief’s date, and the day-count discrepancy is arithmetically checkable from the quoted dates. Confidence is slightly reduced because the filing date itself is not independently verified in the supplied source excerpts.

findings.title: Brief says Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence despite plaintiff witness statement on unsafe scaffold
findings.description: The witness statement was taken by plaintiff’s attorneys and contains evidence that the scaffold was unsafe, that concerns were raised before the collapse, and that Harmon’s foreman dismissed a base-plate concern. That contradicts the assertion that Rivera presented no evidence rebutting reasonable care.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
Statement taken by:  Maria Gutierrez, Esq.
Attorneys for Plaintiff
I raised these concerns with our crew lead, Mark Ellison, before we began work.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The witness statement contains specific facts rebutting reasonable care and is identified as taken by plaintiff’s counsel, so the 'no evidence' statement is strongly undercut. Confidence is not higher because whether evidence was formally 'presented' in the motion record can depend on procedural context not shown in the excerpts.

findings.title: OSHA-compliance framing conflicts with file evidence of unsafe scaffold conditions
findings.description: The file contains no OSHA inspection record proving it was unblemished, and the source documents affirmatively describe unsafe scaffold conditions and pre-incident safety concerns. Those facts conflict with the brief’s framing that Harmon’s safety practices “met or exceeded all regulatory requirements” as to the alleged site safety failures.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Harmon's unblemished OSHA inspection record demonstrates that its safety practices met or exceeded all regulatory requirements, effectively insulating it from tort liability predicated on alleged safety failures at the site.
The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.
Officers observed that portions of the scaffolding in the affected section showed visible rust and wear.
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
Two of the coupling pins on the second tier looked bent or worn.
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The police and witness excerpts directly describe unsafe scaffold conditions, which conflicts with the brief’s broad claim that safety practices met or exceeded all requirements and insulated Harmon from liability. The specific 'unblemished OSHA inspection record' part is unsupported rather than directly disproved by the quoted sources, limiting confidence somewhat.

findings.title: Brief overstates Privette v. Superior Court as an absolute nonliability rule
findings.description: Privette supports a general presumption of nonliability, but the quoted/characterized statement that a hirer is “never liable” is an overstatement and likely not an accurate statement of California law. California recognizes exceptions, including retained control/affirmative contribution and concealed hazard theories; therefore the brief materially overstates Privette by presenting the doctrine as absolute.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The alleged overstatement is strongly supported by the well-known Privette doctrine and its recognized exceptions, but the provided evidence quote is only a pinpoint citation rather than the full proposition. This is the same underlying Privette defect as finding-12, which more directly identifies the doctored/absolute quotation.

findings.title: Legal argument advanced without supporting authority
findings.description: The brief states and applies a rule of law without citing any case, statute, or regulation in support.

evidence.quote: Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted sentence is a legal proposition and the finding is mechanically plausible if, as located by the citation extractor, it appears without any accompanying authority. Confidence is limited because only the isolated sentence is provided, not the full paragraph showing the absence of nearby citations.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Rivera had over eight years of commercial construction experience
findings.description: The police report identifies Rivera as a journeyman scaffolder, but no provided document states that he had “over eight years of experience in commercial construction.” Because the experience-duration component is a specific record-checkable fact with no source support, the claim is unsupported as stated.
findings.brief_location: III.C. Rivera Assumed the Risk Inherent in His Trade

evidence.quote: Rivera is a journeyman scaffolder with over eight years of experience in commercial construction.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The unsupported component is clear in the brief quote, but the evidence does not include the police report or other source excerpts showing only journeyman status and no years-of-experience statement. Confidence therefore depends on the agent’s negative review of the file.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Apex was a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon
findings.description: The documents support that Rivera was employed by Apex and that Apex personnel were acting as the scaffolding crew, but they do not state that Apex was licensed or that Apex was retained by Harmon. Because those specific record-checkable qualifications are asserted as established facts without documentary support, the full claim is unsupported.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 2

evidence.quote: Rivera was employed by Apex Staffing Solutions, a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon for scaffolding assembly and related work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted brief assertion contains the specific unsupported elements, but the evidence list does not include the other file materials demonstrating their absence. Confidence therefore depends on the cross-document agent’s negative review rather than a directly quoted contradiction.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Rivera filed the action on March 10, 2023
findings.description: None of the provided source documents includes the complaint, a filing stamp, docket entry, or other record establishing the action’s filing date. This is a record-checkable filing-date claim, but the supplied sources provide no basis for it.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 6

evidence.quote: Rivera filed the instant action on March 10, 2023.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s filing-date assertion is quoted, but no affirmative source document is provided showing that the case file lacks a complaint, docket, or filing stamp. The finding is reasonable as a negative-record-support issue, but it is not mechanically proved by the evidence excerpt alone.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several serious record and authority defects, led by repeated contradictions of the file materials. It states the incident occurred on March 14, 2021 in the introduction, statement of facts, and time-bar section, while the medical, police, and witness documents all place the incident on March 12, 2021. It also asserts Rivera lacked required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, although the police report and witness statement say he wore a hard hat and harness and that the lanyard detached because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold. The time-bar section is internally inconsistent: its heading says the claims are time-barred, but its own calculation concedes a filing within the two-year window. The brief’s Privette quotation appears overstated as an absolute “never liable” rule, and its Seabright discussion overstates that authority. In addition, eight cited authorities could not be located or confirmed, with varying confidence, including Kellerman and several construction-themed footnote cases.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that, under California law, a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that the Supreme Court held that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that summary judgment is appropriate for a hirer where the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.full_citation: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citations.quoted_text: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that documented full OSHA compliance creates a rebuttable presumption that an employer met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.full_citation: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Privette v. Superior Court as a leading California Supreme Court decision reported at 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993); the reporter, court, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor’s employees, who are covered by workers’ compensation. It is the foundation of the California “Privette doctrine” limiting hirer liability, subject to later-recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition—that a hirer is presumptively not liable for injuries to an independent contractor’s employees performing the contracted work—is consistent with Privette and its progeny. The brief should not omit that the presumption is rebuttable in recognized situations, including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazardous conditions, and related exceptions developed in cases such as Hooker and Kinsman.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to Privette, which is a real California Supreme Court case. I cannot verify the exact page text, but the underlying authority exists.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette generally bars hirer liability to an independent contractor’s employee under the peculiar-risk doctrine, based in part on workers’ compensation and delegation principles. Later California Supreme Court cases recognize exceptions to the general rule.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Privette supports a general presumption of nonliability, but the quoted/characterized statement that a hirer is “never liable” is an overstatement and likely not an accurate statement of California law. California recognizes exceptions, including retained control/affirmative contribution and concealed hazard theories; therefore the brief materially overstates Privette by presenting the doctrine as absolute.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this Ninth Circuit case, and there are strong fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is more consistent with late-1980s Federal Reporter volumes than a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation, and the quoted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspiciously broad and tailored. I am not aware of a general Ninth Circuit/California rule creating a rebuttable presumption of due care from OSHA compliance.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because the case appears likely fabricated and I do not know it as a real authority, I cannot assess support. Substantively, the proposition is suspect under California law: safety-code violations can be relevant or support negligence-per-se concepts in some circumstances, but compliance generally does not automatically create a presumption of reasonable care insulating a defendant from negligence liability.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California negligence law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), as a California Supreme Court Privette-line case; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer generally delegates to an independent contractor the duty to comply with workplace safety requirements, including applicable Cal-OSHA regulations, and therefore the hirer was not liable to the contractor’s employee for the contractor’s failure to comply with those requirements.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright is about delegation of workplace-safety duties under the Privette doctrine, not a general evidentiary rule that compliance with statutory safety requirements is “highly probative” of due care. The brief’s broader use—especially the claim that an unblemished OSHA record effectively insulates Harmon from tort liability—overstates and mischaracterizes Seabright.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a real California statute governing limitations periods for personal-injury and wrongful-death type claims.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The proposition that California personal-injury claims are subject to a two-year limitations period is supported. However, the brief’s broader heading that Rivera’s claims are time-barred is undermined by its own dates: a March 10, 2023 filing for a March 14, 2021 injury appears to fall within two years, absent some different accrual issue.
